coredata.dc:title: Fabrication of a novel reusable nanozyme by immobilizing Co-doped carbon dots on nanocellulose aerogels for efficient dyes degradation

coredata.dc:description: Carbon dot-based nanozymes have gained significant attention, but their application in dye degradation remains limited due to low activity and challenges in recovery and reuse. To overcome these limitations, high peroxidase-active Co-doped carbon dots (CoCDs) with surface amines were synthesized via hydrothermal method and immobilized onto TEMPO-oxidized cellulose nanofibrils (TOCNF) aerogels using EDC/NHS coupling. For the first time, this study investigates the dye degradation efficiency of CDs nanozyme. CoCDs with 1.456 % Co content exhibited excellent peroxidase-like activity with favorable kinetics (Km = 1.746 mM, Vmax = 22.05 × 10−8 Ms−1 for TMB and Km = 1.542 mM, Vmax = 23.54 × 10−8 Ms−1 for H2O2). The immobilized CoCDs enhanced the structural stability, and shape recovery of aerogels, even after multiple compressions. CoCDs/TOCNF aerogel with maximum CoCDs (114.7 mg/g) exhibited superior degradation efficiency for various dyes, including methyl orange (MO), methylene blue (MB), and rhodamine B (RhB), with rate constants of 12.7 × 10−3, 17.4 × 10−3, and 12.2 × 10−3 min−1, respectively. Mechanistic studies revealed the major role of hydroxyl radicals in dye degradation. The aerogels exhibited exceptional recyclability with all dyes. This stable and recyclable nanozyme with high catalytic activity presents a promising avenue for environmental remediation.

UV–vis absorption spectra of the TMB with various systems; Inset: corresponding visual color changes of the TMB solution (a); Influence of various reaction parameters: pH value (b), temperature (c), amount of CoCDs (d) on POD-like activity; Steady-state kinetic analysis of CoCDs: Michaelis–Menten curves for TMB (e) and H2O2 (f); Enzyme-mimicking activity of CoCDs with and without ROS scavengers (g); ESR spectra demonstrating •OH (h) and •O2 − (i) generation in the presence of H2O2 + DMPO and CoCDs + H2O2 + DMPO systems; Schematic illustration showing the possible pathway of POD-like activity for CoCDs nanozyme (j).

Schematic representation showing the steps involved in the preparation of CoCDs/TOCNF aerogels (a); UV absorption spectra for the collected raffinate (diluted 20 times) from the washing of CoCDs/TOCNF aerogels (inserted image is the standard curve and equation obtained from CoCDs concentration vs absorbance) (b); Calculated CoCDs grafting content on TOCNF aerogels based on UV absorption and gravimetric methods (c); Appearances of CoCDs0.3/TOCNF aerogel at transverse and longitudinal cuts (d).

The zeta-potentials of CoCDs (a) and TOCNF (b) aqueous suspensions; XPS survey scans of TOCNF aerogel, CoCDs and CoCDs0.3/TOCNF aerogel (c); High-resolution N1s XPS spectra of CoCDs and CoCDs0.3/TOCNF aerogel (d); FT-IR spectra of CoCDs, TOCNF aerogel and CoCDs0.3/TOCNF aerogel (e); XRD patterns of TOCNF and CoCDs0.3/TOCNF aerogels (f).

Comparison of MO degradation under different conditions, inset: the corresponding photographs (a); Time-dependent degradation absorption spectra of MO in the presence of H2O2 + CoCDs0.3/TOCNF aerogel (b); The effect of H2O2 concentration on MO degradation (c); The effect of temperature on MO degradation: inset showing the Plot of lnk vs 1/T (d); MO degradation with various CoCDs/TOCNF aerogels (e) and corresponding pseudo-first-order kinetic plots (f).

Comparison of MB degradation under different conditions, Inset: the corresponding photographs (a); Time-dependent degradation absorption spectra of MB in the presence of H2O2 + CoCDs0.3/TOCNF aerogel (b); Comparison of RhB degradation under different conditions, Inset: the corresponding photographs (c); Time-dependent degradation absorption spectra of RhB in the presence of H2O2 + CoCDs0.3/TOCNF aerogel (d); Effect of different scavengers on MO degradation (e); Schematic illustration of dyes degradation with CoCDs/TOCNF aerogels (f).

Recyclability of CoCDs0.3/TOCNF aerogel for degradation of MO (a), MB (b) and RhB (c); Transverse (d) and longitudinal (e) cut SEM images of 5 times reused CoCDs0.3/TOCNF aerogel; MO degradation in real water samples (f) (1 mg/mL MO stock solution was added to pH 4.00 buffer solution prepared with real water samples and without pH adjusted real water samples).

head.ce:title: Fabrication of a novel reusable nanozyme by immobilizing Co-doped carbon dots on nanocellulose aerogels for efficient dyes degradation

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Carbon dot-based nanozymes have gained significant attention, but their application in dye degradation remains limited due to low activity and challenges in recovery and reuse. To overcome these limitations, high peroxidase-active Co-doped carbon dots (CoCDs) with surface amines were synthesized via hydrothermal method and immobilized onto TEMPO-oxidized cellulose nanofibrils (TOCNF) aerogels using EDC/NHS coupling. For the first time, this study investigates the dye degradation efficiency of CDs nanozyme. CoCDs with 1.456 % Co content exhibited excellent peroxidase-like activity with favorable kinetics (Km = 1.746 mM, Vmax = 22.05 × 10−8 Ms−1 for TMB and Km = 1.542 mM, Vmax = 23.54 × 10−8 Ms−1 for H2O2). The immobilized CoCDs enhanced the structural stability, and shape recovery of aerogels, even after multiple compressions. CoCDs/TOCNF aerogel with maximum CoCDs (114.7 mg/g) exhibited superior degradation efficiency for various dyes, including methyl orange (MO), methylene blue (MB), and rhodamine B (RhB), with rate constants of 12.7 × 10−3, 17.4 × 10−3, and 12.2 × 10−3 min−1, respectively. Mechanistic studies revealed the major role of hydroxyl radicals in dye degradation. The aerogels exhibited exceptional recyclability with all dyes. This stable and recyclable nanozyme with high catalytic activity presents a promising avenue for environmental remediation.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:para: Nanozymes, nanomaterials mimicking enzyme-like catalytic properties, offer a promising approach to degrading organic pollutants in wastewater [1–3]. Compared to natural enzymes, nanozymes exhibit several advantages, including easier synthesis, lower cost, higher stability, and the potential for multifunctional activities [4–6]. These advantages effectively address the limitations of natural enzymes in wastewater treatment applications [3]. The discovery of peroxidase (POD)-like activity in Fe3O4 nanoparticles [7] spurred the development and extensive research of various nanomaterials with enzyme-like activity for applications in diagnosis, sensing, antimicrobial, and organic pollutants degradation [8–10].

Among all nanozymes, carbon dots (CDs) have received significant attention due to their biocompatibility, ease of modification, low-cost synthesis, and diverse catalytic capabilities [11–15]. While current research on CD-based nanozymes has primarily focused on applications in sensing, therapies, antimicrobial treatments, and biomedicine [16–19], their potential for degrading organic pollutants remains largely unexplored. Although substantial progress has been made in the photocatalytic and NaBH4-assisted degradation of organic pollutants using CDs [20–22], the enzyme-mimicking catalytic properties CD-based nanozymes for pollutant degradation are still underutilized. Compared to photocatalytic and borohydride-assisted methods, nanozyme-based dye degradation offers key advantages, including the elimination of the need for UV light or complex experimental setups, as well as the avoidance of toxic reagents. Therefore, exploring the potential of CD-based nanozymes for organic pollutant degradation could lead to eco-friendly advancements in wastewater treatment.

Unlike metal or metal oxide-based nanozymes, CDs exhibit lower catalytic activity due to their limited number of active sites. This catalytic activity can be enhanced through metal doping, which introduces additional active sites and alters charge distribution [23–25]. Among various metals, Co doping is particularly promising due to its intrinsic POD-like activity. For instance, Lu et al. [26] fabricated Co-doped CDs using a pyrolysis method for glucose detection and tumor catalytic therapy. Similarly, Zhang et al. [23] constructed Co/N-doped CDs via a solvothermal method for detecting total antioxidant capacity. Su et al. [27] explored the pH independence and substrate selectivity of Co, N-doped CDs for biosensing and bioimaging applications.

A major drawback of CDs-based nanozymes in dye degradation is their difficulty in recovery and reuse—critical attributes for practical catalytic applications, stemmed from their small size and high-water solubility [28–30]. To address these limitations, researchers are exploring immobilizing CDs nanozymes on carrier materials, which enhances their stability, simplifies separation, and enables reuse [13,31]. From a sustainability perspective, using natural polymers is preferred over synthetic ones.

Nanocellulose, derived from plant materials, is an attractive carrier for immobilizing nanozymes due to its excellent mechanical properties, abundant hydroxyl groups, biodegradability, and high availability [32–34]. Nanocellulose also offers the unique advantage of enabling the construction of various architectures, such as films, fibers, hydrogels, and aerogels [35,36]. Aerogels, in particular, are well-suited for loading and anchoring large quantities of nanozymes due to their high interconnected porosity and large surface area [37–39]. They also prevent aggregation of nanozymes and facilitate easy separation from the reaction medium. As a result, nanozyme-aerogel composites hold great potential as reusable catalysts for the degradation of organic pollutants.

This study seeks to pioneer the application of CD-based nanozymes in dye degradation. Co-doped CDs (CoCDs) with enhanced POD-like activity were synthesized via a hydrothermal method and subsequently immobilized onto TEMPO-oxidized cellulose nanofibril (TOCNF) aerogels using an efficient EDC/NHS coupling reaction. This novel approach aims to address critical challenges associated with CDs-nanozymes, such as recovery and reuse, by leveraging the unique properties of nanocellulose aerogels. The immobilization not only enabled facile recovery and prevented nanozyme elution during dye degradation but also enhanced the aerogel's mechanical properties while retaining its porous structure. This reusable nanozyme-aerogel composite represents a significant step toward sustainable and efficient wastewater treatment solutions.

Bleached hardwood Kraft pulp with 85.2 % cellulose, 14.2 % hemicellulose, and 0.6 % lignin was used for TOCNF preparation. 2, 2, 6, 6-tetramethylpyperidine-1-oxyl (TEMPO, 98 %), sodium hydroxide (NaOH), sodium bromide (NaBr), sodium hypochlorite (NaClO), CoCl2·6H2O (98 %), branched polyethylenimine (PEI; Mw = 800, >95 %), 3,3′,5,5′-Tetramethylbenzidine (TMB, ≥98 %) and 5,5-dimethyl-1-pyrroline N-oxide (DMPO) were purchased from Sigma Aldrich (St Louis, MO, USA). Citric acid (CA, 99.5 %), hydrogen peroxide (H2O2, 30 %), 1-Ethyl-3-(3-dimethylaminopropyl) carbodiimide hydrochloride (EDC, 99 %), N-hydroxysuccinimide (NHS, 98 %), methyl orange (MO), methylene blue trihydrate (MB), rhodamine B (RhB), isopropyl alcohol (IPA), p-benzoquinone (BQ) and sodium azide (NaN3) were purchased from Daejung chemicals (Siheung-si, Gyeonggi Province, South Korea). TMB stock solution was prepared with anhydrous ethanol and dyes stock solutions were prepared with deionized water (DW). All chemicals were of analytical reagent grade and used as received without any further treatment.

CoCDs were synthesized following previously reported methods with minor modifications [37,40]. Briefly, 1 g of CA and 0.375 g of CoCl2·6H2O were completely dissolved in 30 mL of deionized water (CA:Co molar ratio is 10:3). Subsequently, 4.2 g of PEI was added and stirred for 1 h. The resulting solution was transferred into a 50 mL Teflon-lined stainless-steel autoclave and subjected to hydrothermal treatment at 180 °C for 20 h. After cooling, the solution was dialyzed for 2 days against deionized water using a dialysis membrane (MWCO 3.5 kDa). Finally, the CoCDs were obtained by rotary evaporation and subsequent drying. Similar procedures were employed to prepare CoCDs with different CA:Co molar ratios 10:1, 10:2, 10:4 and 10:5. Un-doped CDs were prepared using a similar method without adding CoCl2·6H2O.

The Enzyme-mimicking activity of CoCDs was evaluated by monitoring the oxidation of the chromogenic substrate, TMB, in the presence of H2O2. In a typical assay, 100 μL of TMB (20 mM) and 100 μL of H2O2 (20 mM), followed by 200 μL of CoCDs (1 mg/mL), were added to 1600 μL of acetate buffer (0.1 M, pH 4.00). The reaction mixture was incubated at 45 °C for 20 min, and the absorbance of the oxidized TMB (ox-TMB) was measured at 652 nm using a UV–visible spectrophotometer.

Steady-state kinetic assays were conducted by fixing the concentration of one substrate while varying the concentration of the other. The absorbance change at 652 nm was recorded for 5 min in time course mode. The obtained absorbance values were converted into concentration using the Beer-Lambert law with a molar extinction coefficient (ε) of 39,000 M−1 cm−1 for ox-TMB. The kinetic parameters, Vmax and Km, were determined by fitting the reaction velocity data to the Michaelis-Menten equation:
V =
V max
S
/
K m
+
S
where V and Vmax represent the initial and maximal reaction velocities, Km is the Michaelis-Menten constant, and [S] is the substrate concentration.

The enzyme-mimicking activity was investigated through reactive oxygen species (ROS) scavenging experiments and electron spin resonance (ESR) spectroscopy. For the scavenging experiments, IPA, BQ, and NaN3 were employed as scavengers for •OH, •O2 − and 1O2, respectively. The procedure was identical to the above enzyme-mimicking activity assay, except that scavengers were added to the reaction system.

For ESR analysis, DMPO was used as a trapping agent for •OH and •O2 −. In a typical experiment, 100 μL of CoCDs (1 mg/mL) was added to 900 μL of an acetate buffer solution (pH 4.00, 0.1 M) containing 5 mM H2O2 and 0.1 M DMPO (dissolved in methanol). The mixture was allowed to react for 10 min before analysis. ESR measurements were carried out on a JEOL spectrometer (JES-X320).

TOCNF was prepared following a previously reported method with slight modifications [31]. Initially, 10 g of dry hardwood Kraft pulp was suspended in 1 L of deionized water. Subsequently, 0.16 g of TEMPO and 1 g of NaBr were added. The oxidation reaction proceeded at room temperature with mild agitation while 1 M NaClO (3 mM/g pulp) was added dropwise. The pH was maintained at 10.00 by adding 0.5 M NaOH or 0.1 M HCl. When the pH stabilized, 100 mL ethanol was added to terminate the reaction. The pH of the suspension was then adjusted to 8.50 using 0.1 M HCl. The obtained product was purified by dialyzing against distilled water for two days, with the water replaced three times daily. Subsequently, the suspension was blended at 35,000 rpm for 30 min using a high-speed blender (model HR-3752, Koninklijke, Philips). Finally, the resulting gel-like suspension was centrifuged at 10,017 g-force for 10 min, and the supernatant was collected as the TOCNF suspension.

Pristine cylindrical TOCNF aerogels were fabricated through freeze-drying. Briefly, a 1 wt% TOCNF suspension was dispensed into a 24-well plate (each well 15 mm diameter × 17 mm height) and frozen at −18 °C for 24 h, followed by freeze-drying at −50 °C for 48 h using an OPERON freeze dryer. Then, one aerogel was immersed in a 20 mL aqueous solution of CoCDs, along with the addition of 120 mg EDC and 460 mg NHS [41]. Afterwards, the reactor was subjected to a vacuum for 3 h in vacuum chamber followed by shaking for 12 h at 150 rpm in a shaking incubator (VS-101Si, VISION Scientific Co. LTD.). The resulting CoCDs/TOCNF aerogel was washed multiple times with DW in the same shaking incubator (30 min per wash). Finally, the aerogel was freeze-dried. CoCDs/TOCNF aerogels with various amounts of CoCDs (0.05, 0.1, 0.2, 0.3, and 0.4 wt%) were prepared and labeled as CoCDs-x/TOCNF (where x represents the treated CoCDs concentration). An undoped CDs-grafted aerogel (CDs0.3/TOCNF aerogel) was also prepared for comparison. All prepared aerogels were stored in a desiccator for subsequent analysis.

The morphologies of CoCDs and TOCNF were examined using transmission electron microscopy (TEM; JEOL, JEM-2100F; Tokyo, Japan; operated at 200 kV). The crystal structure of the aerogels and CoCDs was characterized by X-ray diffraction (XRD) using an X'pert PRO MPD X-diffractometer (PAN analytical: Netherlands) with Cu-Kα radiation of λ = 1.5418 Å (40 kV, 30 mA) with a scanning rate of 5° min−1. Surface chemical composition was determined by X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) analysis using a Thermo Fisher Scientific spectrometer with an Al Kα X-ray source. The morphology and elemental composition of the aerogels were investigated by scanning electron microscopy (SEM, Hitachi S-4800) equipped with energy-dispersive X-ray spectroscopy (EDS). To preserve the original aerogel structure, samples were immersed in liquid nitrogen, sectioned, mounted on SEM stubs using carbon tape, coated with iridium, and then imaged. Optical properties of CoCDs aqueous solution (0.2 mg/mL) were characterized using an OPTIZEN-POP UV–visible spectrophotometer and a Hitachi fluorescence spectrometer. The zeta potentials of TOCNF and CoCDs aqueous suspensions were measured with a Malvern Zetasizer Nano ZS. Attenuated total reflectance Fourier transform infrared (ATR-FTIR) spectra were collected in the 4000–400 cm−1 range using a Perkin Elmer Frontier spectrometer. Co content and its leaching were quantified by inductively coupled plasma optical emission spectrometry (ICP-OES, PerkinElmer Optima 8300). For this, samples were digested in 10 % HNO₃ and filtered through a 0.45 μm membrane filter.

The CoCDs grafting content on TOCNF aerogels was determined by collecting and diluting (20 times) the raffinate (including washing residues) and measuring UV absorbance at 352 nm. The grafting content was calculated using the following equation [37].
G CD
mg / g

V i

m TOCNF
where Vi and Ci are the initial volume and concentration of CoCDs; Vr and Cr represent the volume and concentration of the raffinate CoCDs; and mTOCNF is the mass of the pure TOCNF aerogel. In this study, Vi was 20 mL, mTOCNF was approximately 0.035 g, and Ci ranged from 0.05 to 0.4 wt%. Vr was measured after the raffinate collection, and Cr was calculated from the UV absorbance using a CoCDs standard curve.

Aerogel densities were determined by measuring mass and volume. The actual density (ρ) was calculated using the formula
ρ =
m v
where ρ is the aerogel density, m is the mass, and v is the volume (obtained by v = l × w × h, with l, w, and h representing length, diameter, and height). The dimensions and weight of the aerogels were measured using a digital caliper and an analytical balance. Porosity (P) was calculated using the equation
P

where P is the aerogel porosity, ρ is the density, and ρs is the skeletal density of cellulose (CNF at 1.6 g/cm3) [37].

The shape recoverability and structural stability of the aerogels were evaluated by compression tests in DW using a 200 g calibration weight. For structural stability, aerogels undergo multiple loading-unloading compression cycles.

The catalytic performance of CoCDs/TOCNF aerogels was evaluated by degrading MO, MB, and RhB dyes, with absorbance changes recorded at 474 nm, 665 nm, and 555 nm, respectively. In a typical experiment, a dye stock solution (1 mg/mL) was added to a flask containing acetate buffer (pH 4.0). Subsequently, the aerogel was immersed in the dye solution, followed by the addition of 30 % H2O2. The sealed reactor was placed in a shaking incubator, and the reaction was carried out at 45 °C for 210 min. The final concentrations of dye, H2O2, and total volume were maintained at 15 mg/L, 1 M, and 40 mL, respectively. At predetermined intervals, 2 mL of the solution was collected, and the absorbance was monitored using a UV spectrophotometer. All experiments were conducted in triplicate, and the degradation rate was determined by the formula:
Dye
degradation rate =
C 0
−
C t

× 100 % =
A 0
−
A t

× 100 %
where ct is the concentration of the dye solution at a given reaction time, c0 is the initial concentration of the dye solution, at is the absorbance of the dye solution at a given reaction time, and a0 is the initial absorbance of the dye solution [42].
As heterogeneous catalysis typically follows Langmuir–Hinshelwood kinetics, the use of low dye concentration in this study allowed the approximation of a pseudo-first-order kinetic model:

The real-world applicability of CoCDs/TOCNF aerogels for MO dye degradation was assessed using river and lake water. Water samples were collected from Seoksacheon Stream, a local tributary of the Bukhangang River, and a campus lake, filtered (Advantec No. 2 filter paper), and used to prepare acetate buffer (pH 4.0). Dye stock solution (1 mg/mL) was added to the buffered water. Degradation was also evaluated in unbuffered filtered water to assess performance under unaltered environmental conditions. All experiments followed the previously described protocol.

CoCDs were synthesized using a hydrothermal method (Fig. 1a), employing CA as the carbon source, CoCl2·6H2O as the dopant metal ion source, and PEI to modify the surface with amino groups. It has been reported that doping metal atoms into CDs not only increases the number of catalytically active centers but also influences their charge distribution [43]. Considering the known catalytic effect of Co ions, they were used as the dopant. To enhance the POD-like activity, the Co content was tuned by varying the precursor mole ratios. As depicted in Fig. 1b, POD-like activity increased twofold as the precursor mole ratio increased from 10:1 to 10:3. Beyond 10:3, no significant change in activity was observed. Therefore, CoCDs prepared at a 10:3 ratio were selected for further characterization and immobilization.

TEM image of CoCDs (Fig. 1c) revealed a spherical and monodisperse nanoparticle morphology without any noticeable aggregation. HRTEM results (inset in Fig. 1c) indicated that the CoCDs possessed a crystalline structure with a noticeable lattice spacing of approximately 0.21 nm, consistent with the (100) planes of graphitic carbon [37]. Statistical analysis of approximately 100 particles from multiple images revealed CoCDs sizes ranging from 3.50 to 7.50 nm, with an average size of 5.15 ± 1.15 nm (Fig. 1d).

Fig. 1e shows the absorption, excitation, and emission spectra of CoCDs. The absorption spectrum exhibited two characteristic peaks at 240 nm (π-π transition of the aromatic ring) and 352 nm (n-π transition of CO and CN bonds) [40]. The CoCDs aqueous solution appeared transparent and brownish-yellow under daylight and emitted bright blue fluorescence under 365 nm UV illumination (inset) [44]. The maximum emission of CoCDs was observed at 435 nm when excited at 350 nm. As shown in Fig. S1a, CoCDs exhibited excitation-dependent emission behavior with a red shift and an initial increase followed by a decrease in emission intensity as the excitation wavelength (λex) increased from 340 to 460 nm. This phenomenon is likely attributed to the presence of various surface energy traps (SETs) on the CDs [13]. The XRD pattern of CoCDs (Fig. S1b) showed a broad peak at around 26.24°, corresponding to the (002) lattice plane of graphite [37]. FTIR analysis (Fig. 1f) revealed the presence of OH and NH groups on the CoCD surface, originating from the precursor materials, indicated by a broad peak in the 3000–3600 cm−1 region. Intense peaks at 1557 and 1650 cm−1 are attributed to the stretching vibrations of NH2 and CONH bonds, respectively [37,40]. These observations suggest that during CoCD formation, the characteristic carboxylic acid peaks in CA (1692 and 1743 cm−1) transformed into the carbonyl stretching frequencies of amide groups. Furthermore, a peak around 562 cm−1 is associated with the stretching vibration of CoN, derived from CoCl2·6H2O, confirming the successful cobalt doping and the creation of active sites for the CoCDs nanozyme [26].

The chemical composition and valence states were investigated by XPS. Four characteristic binding energy peaks appeared in the XPS survey scan of CoCDs (Fig. 1g), corresponding to C (284 eV), O (531 eV), N (400 eV) and Co (780 eV) elements. Appearance of Co 2p peak illustrated the successful doping of Co into the CDs. High-resolution Co 2p XPS spectrum (Fig. 1h) exhibited distinctive peaks of Co 2p3/2 and Co 2p1/2 at 779.53 eV and 795.22 eV, respectively, ascribed to its spin-orbit splitting [44]. In addition, the binding energies at 783.41 eV and 800.66 eV are ascribed to their shake-up resonance transitions [27]. These results indicate the presence of Co (II) ions in prepared CoCDs, which provide the structural basis for the enzymatic reactions [26,44]. Moreover, the high-resolution XPS scan of N 1s exhibited peaks at 398.52 and 400.96 eV indicating the presence of CNC and NH2 groups (Fig. S1c), respectively [40], further confirming the presence of amino functional groups on CoCDs surface, which can bind to the carboxyl groups of TOCNF. Co content in the CoCDs was found to be 1.456 % by ICP-OES analysis. The Co content was higher compared to the previous report [26], which is beneficial for achieving high catalytic activity. These findings confirm the successful synthesis of CoCDs with Co integrated into the CDs structure.

The enzyme-mimicking activity of CoCDs was assessed by the oxidation of TMB to blue-colored ox-TMB, which has an absorption peak at 652 nm. The absorption spectra (Fig. 2a) and the color changes (inset of Fig. 2a) of various systems were recorded. The absorbance of TMB at 652 nm was negligible in the presence of CoCDs alone. However, when CoCDs were combined with H2O2, a clear absorption peak appeared at 652 nm, and the solution color changed to blue [45], indicating that CoCDs exhibited POD-like activity. No absorbance or color change was observed when TMB or TMB + H2O2 were present. Further, to evaluate the crucial role of Co, enzyme-mimicking activity was tested in the presence of un-doped CDs and H2O2. No absorption peak and color change were observed, indicating that un-doped CDs could not catalyze H2O2 to oxidize TMB. Similar results were reported in a previous study [26], confirming the decisive role of Co in the catalytic activity. The effects of several parameters on the POD-like activity were investigated. The activity of CoCDs increased and then decreased with increasing pH, reaching its highest activity at pH 4.00 (Fig. 2b). Similarly, the activity increased and then decreased with rising temperature, with the highest activity observed at 45 °C (Fig. 2c). In terms of CoCDs amount variation, the activity gradually increased with the amount of CoCDs until reaching a plateau, with an optimized amount of 200 μg (Fig. 2d). CoCDs prepared in five batches showed consistent activity (Fig. S2a), and their POD-like activity remained stable after long-term storage at room temperature (Fig. S2b), indicating excellent reproducibility and stability.

The steady-state kinetics of CoCDs were further investigated by varying the concentrations of TMB and H2O2 to fit the Michaelis-Menten equation and obtain the catalytic constants Km and Vmax. Km characterizes the affinity of the nanozyme for the reaction substrate, with a smaller Km value indicating a higher affinity and vice versa. Vmax represents the maximum reaction rate, with a higher Vmax indicating higher catalytic activity of the nanozyme [46]. Fig. 2e and f show the Michaelis-Menten curves with TMB and H2O2 as substrates, respectively. The fitting results indicate that Km = 1.746 mM and Vmax = 22.05 × 10−8 Ms−1 with TMB as the substrate, and Km = 1.542 mM and Vmax = 23.54 × 10−8 Ms−1 with H2O2 as substrate, respectively. These values were comparable to or even better than natural horseradish peroxidase (HRP) and other Co-doped nanozymes (Table S1). The Km value for CoCDs with H2O2 as a substrate was lower than that of HRP, indicating a stronger binding affinity of CoCDs toward H2O2. This stronger affinity is desirable for achieving remarkable catalytic activity. Additionally, compared to HRP the much higher Vmax values of CoCDs for both TMB and H2O2 further confirm the outstanding catalytic activity of CoCDs.

To investigate the catalytic mechanism of CoCDs, the role of ROS was examined (Fig. 2g). IPA, BQ, and NaN3 were employed as •OH, •O2 −, and 1O2 scavengers, respectively. Compared to the blank, POD-like activity gradually decreased with increasing IPA concentration, indicating a significant contribution of •OH radicals. In contrast, the presence of BQ had only a slight inhibitory effect at 2 mM, suggesting a minor role of •O2 −. No significant decrease in POD-like activity was observed with increasing NaN3 concentration, ruling out a major contribution of 1O2. Further confirmation of ROS generation was obtained through ESR spectroscopy. As depicted in Fig. 2h and i, signals for both •OH and •O2 − were detected only when CoCDs, H2O2, and the spin trap DMPO were present together. The weak •O2 − signal suggests that CoCDs can catalyze the decomposition of H2O2 to produce •O2 −. XPS analysis confirmed the presence of Co(II) ions in CoCDs, which exhibit excellent Fenton reaction activity. This catalytic activity drives the decomposition of H2O2, generating •OH and •O2 −, ultimately leading to the oxidation of chromogenic substrates [26]. Based on these findings, a schematic illustration was proposed to elucidate the possible pathway of POD-like activity in CoCDs nanozymes (Fig. 2j).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Synthesis and characterization of TOCNF and CoCDs/TOCNF aerogels.

ce:section.ce:para: TOCNF suspension was prepared from hardwood pulp using TEMPO-oxidation followed by blending. During this process, regioselective oxidation of the primary hydroxyl groups at the 6th position to carboxylate groups occurred. This modification created electrostatic repulsion between adjacent nanofibrils, and stabilized the TOCNF suspension. The carboxylate content was estimated to be 1.03 ± 0.03 mM/g by conductometric titration, consistent with previous reports [31]. The length and diameter were measured using TEM and calculated to be 0.3–1.0 μm and 3.00–8.00 nm, respectively (Fig. S3). TOCNF aerogels were then prepared by freeze-drying the suspension in a 24-well plate.

The schematic illustration for CoCDs/TOCNF aerogels preparation is shown in Fig. 3a, including the pure TOCNF aerogel preparation. The TOCNF aerogels were immersed in varying concentrations of CoCDs solutions along with the addition of EDC and NHS. A vacuum infiltration technique was employed to ensure a homogeneous distribution of CoCDs within the aerogel pores. Subsequently, the aerogel-containing reactors underwent a 12 h rotation at 150 rpm in a shaking incubator to facilitate the crosslinking reaction. Afterward, the aerogels were washed with water to eliminate any unreacted CoCDs, EDC, and NHS. Notably, during the washing cycles, the raffinate's color transitioned from brown to colorless, accompanied by the absence of fluorescence under 365 nm UV light (Fig. S4). After the fourth washing cycle, the absence of fluorescence in the washing solution confirmed the complete removal of unbound CoCDs. Finally, the prepared CoCDs/TOCNF aerogels were freeze-dried, resulting in a distinctive yellow-brown coloration, contrasting sharply with the pristine white appearance of pure TOCNF. The exceptional lightweight nature of aerogels was visually demonstrated by placing them on grass flowers (Fig. S5). The grafting content of CoCDs on TOCNF aerogels was determined by UV–visible absorption and gravimetric measurements (Fig. 3b and c). Fig. 3b shows the absorption spectrum of the raffinates (including washing residues and 20 times dilution) collected after the fabrication of CoCDs/TOCNF aerogels at different concentrations. Based on the absorption measurement and gravimetric measurement, the CoCDs0.3/TOCNF aerogel had the highest CoCDs grafting content (114.7 mg/g TOCNF aerogel), as further shown in Table S2. This high CoCDs grafting content is crucial for generating more ROS and further degrading organic pollutants. Cross-sectional observations of the vertically and transversely cut CoCDs/TOCNF aerogel exhibited the same color as the surface (Fig. 3d), indicating uniform CoCDs grafting throughout the aerogel.

To investigate if electrostatic interaction or covalent crosslinking occurred between CoCDs and TOCNF aerogels, the zeta potential of CoCDs solution and TOCNF suspension was studied. As shown in Fig. 4 , CoCDs had a weakly charged surface with a zeta potential of −9.31 mV (Fig. 4a), while TOCNF exhibited a typical negatively charged surface with a zeta potential of −54.2 mV due to TEMPO pretreatment (Fig. 4b). These results suggest that the interaction is a strong covalent linkage between carboxyl and amine groups rather than an electrostatic interaction, similar result was presented in previous report [37]. Furthermore, the possible covalent crosslinking between TOCNF aerogels and CoCDs was investigated using several analytical techniques. In the survey XPS spectrum (Fig. 4c), TOCNF exhibited only C 1s (285 eV) and O 1s (530 eV) peaks, whereas CoCDs0.3/TOCNF exhibited an N 1s peak in addition to the C 1s and O 1s peaks, indicating the presence of CoCDs. Peaks related to Co were not observed due to the low-loading content. In the high-resolution N 1s spectrum (Fig. 4d), the disappearance of the 400.96 eV peak in CoCDs0.3/TOCNF compared to CoCDs confirmed the consumption of amines, providing clear evidence of covalent crosslinking between CoCDs and TOCNF aerogels. FTIR spectrum of TOCNF contained the typical cellulose characteristics peaks along with the presence of carboxyl stretching (COO−) at 1600 cm−1, formed during the TEMPO-oxidation [31]. In the case of the CoCDs0.3/TOCNF aerogel, the appearance of two peaks at 1557 and 1650 cm−1 indicated that the CoCDs were crosslinked with TOCNF carboxyl groups because the CoCDs were surface amine modified (Fig. 4e) [47,48]. The XRD patterns of TOCNF (Fig. 4f) revealed characteristic cellulose I peaks at 2θ around 15.3°, 22.5°, and 35.2°, corresponding to (110), (200), and (004) reflections, respectively. Similar diffraction peaks were observed for CoCDs0.3/TOCNF, and there was no change in crystallinity index values (57.55 % for TOCNF and 57.65 % for CoCDs0.3/TOCNF), indicating that CoCDs did not disturb the crystallinity of TOCNF during cross-linking. Similar results were also observed in previous studies [13]. Overall, these results indicate that the CoCDs were immobilized on TOCNF aerogels through amidation between their surface amino groups and carboxylate groups of TOCNF.

The microstructure of the aerogels was examined by observing transverse and longitudinal cuts using SEM. Both pure TOCNF aerogel (Fig. 5a and b) and CoCDs0.3/TOCNF aerogel (Fig. 5c and d) revealed a porous microstructure, featuring numerous interconnected micropores of varying sizes. The covalently bonded CoCDs on the TOCNF-based skeleton did not induce a structural collapse or closure of micropores in the aerogels, which is crucial for their potential application in wastewater treatment. Furthermore, the porosities and densities of the as-prepared aerogels are calculated and provided in Table S3. The porosity of the aerogels plays a crucial role in wastewater treatment by interacting with pollutants within their structure [49,50]. All CoCDs/TOCNF aerogels exhibited a lightweight and very high porosity (>99 %). Elemental mapping (Fig. 5e) demonstrated the distribution of elements (C, O, N, and Co) in the CoCDs0.3/TOCNF aerogel. N and Co were evenly distributed on the TOCNF layers, indicating the uniform grafting of CoCDs throughout the aerogel.

The shape-memory performance and structural stability of aerogels, as well as leaching experiments are important parameters for recycling experiments. To assess these properties, the aerogels underwent compression tests underwater. As illustrated in Fig. S6, all aerogels including pure TOCNF, were completely restored to their original shape within seconds, even if their deformation was as high as 80 %. However, after six cycles of compression, the TOCNF aerogel structure collapsed (Fig. 5f), while the CoCDs/TOCNF aerogel retained its structure without any damage even after ten cycles of compression (Fig. 5g). The improved structural stability of CoCDs/TOCNF aerogels can be attributed to the covalently linked CoCDs, which act as a crosslinking agent for the porous skeleton of the TOCNF-based aerogel, similar improvements observed in previous report [37]. The robust mechanical stability underwater endows the aerogels with flexibility and recyclability, which is beneficial for wastewater treatment. Additionally, the relative height of the CoCDs/TOCNF aerogel at each cycle is presented in Fig. S7. After 10 compression-recovery cycles, the height of the aerogel decreased by only about 12 %.

The leakage of CoCDs during successive compressions was investigated by measuring the absorption spectrum and taking fluorescence photographs of the residual liquids after the 1st, 5th, and 10th cycles (Fig. S8). To assess leakage, the CoCDs0.3/TOCNF aerogel was placed in 10 mL of water and compressed using a 200 g weight, resulting in approximately 80 % deformation of its height. During the compressions, the liquids were collected for photographing under 365 nm UV radiation and absorption measurement, then the liquid was re-added to the same solution. As shown in Fig. S8, no fluorescence appeared in the residual liquids, and no absorbance peak was observed, further demonstrating the strong grafting of CoCDs on the skeleton of the TOCNF aerogel. Moreover, immersing the crushed CoCDs/TOCNF aerogel in water for 4 h did not reveal any fluorescent behavior in the supernatant (Fig. S9), confirming the absence of CoCDs leakage. These findings highlight the durability and reusability of the CoCDs/TOCNF aerogels.

Encouraged by the excellent POD-like activity of CoCDs and their ability to produce ROS through the Fenton's reaction of Co (II), their application for organic dyes degradation was investigated by grafting them onto TOCNF aerogels to prevent their loss and enable their reusability. Given the negatively charged surface of the aerogels, we initially investigated the degradation of the anionic dye MO as a model contaminant to mitigate potential adsorption interference. As shown in Fig. 6a, control experiments demonstrated negligible dye removal in the presence of either H2O2 (0.74 %) or CoCDs0.3/TOCNF aerogel (3.03 %), or TOCNF aerogel (2.62 %) alone, even after 210 min. This observation indicates that H2O2 alone cannot oxidize MO effectively, and that CoCDs0.3/TOCNF aerogel or TOCNF aerogel alone exhibit insignificant adsorption due to electrostatic repulsion between the negatively charged dye and the aerogel surface. However, in the presence of both CoCDs0.3/TOCNF aerogel and H2O2, 93.07 % of MO was degraded within 210 min, resulting in nearly colorless solutions (inset in Fig. 6a). These findings strongly suggest that the degradation of MO in the presence of CoCDs0.3/TOCNF aerogel and H2O2 is primarily driven by the potent POD-like activity of the CoCDs nanozyme, rather than adsorption-based removal. Furthermore, the absorption spectra at different time intervals were recorded for MO degradation in the presence of CoCDs0.3/TOCNF aerogel and H2O2 (Fig. 6b). The intensity of the absorption peak gradually decreased at 474 nm, and the color of the reaction mixture faded in 210 min (Fig. S10). To evaluate the effect of H2O2 concentration on dyes degradation, MO was degraded with various concentrations of H2O2 (Fig. 6c). The degradation efficiency initially increased with increasing H2O2 concentration from 0.25 M to 1 M, but then stabilized, suggesting that a 1 M H2O2 concentration is optimal for dyes degradation. To calculate the activation energy (Ea) for dye degradation, the temperature effect on MO degradation was studied. As shown in Fig. 6d, the reaction rate increased with increasing temperature from 25 °C to 45 °C, and the calculated Ea using the Arrhenius equation is 33.47 kJ/mol (inset in Fig. 6d). Moreover, to evaluate the effect of CoCDs grafting content on dyes degradation, MO was degraded with different CoCDs grafting aerogels (Fig. 6e). Due to the high CoCDs content, CoCDs0.3/TOCNF aerogel exhibited the highest degradation efficiency (93.07 %). The rate constants (k) increased from 4.7 × 10−3 min−1 to 12.7 × 10−3 min−1 when the CoCDs content increased from 0.05 to 0.3 %, and further increase caused a minor decrease in MO degradation (Fig. 6f). This increase was expected since the active sites increased with CoCDs loading, resulting in the production of more ROS.

To demonstrate the versatility of CoCDs/TOCNF aerogels in wastewater treatment, we further investigated the degradation of other commonly used dyes, namely MB and RhB. Similar to MO degradation, when both CoCDs0.3/TOCNF aerogel and H2O2 were present, the degradation efficiencies reached 97.43 % for MB and 92.34 % for RhB after 210 min (Fig. 7a and c), respectively. To assess the contribution of adsorption, we conducted experiments in the presence of either H2O2, CoCDs0.3/TOCNF aerogel, or TOCNF aerogel alone. As shown in Fig. 7a and c, the adsorption contribution of CoCDs0.3/TOCNF aerogel was limited, with 7.73 % for MB and 5.51 % for RhB after 210 min. In contrast, TOCNF aerogel exhibited higher adsorption, with 18.19 % of MB and 7.58 % of RhB adsorbed after 210 min. Although both dyes are cationic, RhB adsorption on TOCNF aerogel is lower than MB adsorption. This can be attributed to the negative zeta potential of RhB [46,51], which reduces electrostatic attraction to the negatively charged TOCNF surface. Furthermore, despite the overall negative surface charge of CoCDs0.3/TOCNF aerogel, dye adsorption was lower compared to TOCNF. This is likely due to the involvement of most carboxyl groups of TOCNF in amidation reactions with the amino groups of CoCDs, reducing their availability for dye binding. Additionally, direct oxidation by H2O2 was minimal, with negligible oxidation of MB (0.58 %) and slight oxidation of RhB (5.12 %). These results strongly suggest that catalytic degradation is the primary mechanism for dye removal, rather than adsorption. The ROS generated by the POD-like activity of CoCDs likely play a crucial role in directly breaking down dye molecules in the solution or degrading dyes that may have adsorbed onto the aerogel surface during the 210-min degradation process. This dual action significantly enhances the overall degradation efficiency while minimizing the contribution of adsorption. Pseudo-first-order kinetics analysis revealed rate constants of 17.4 × 10−3 min−1 for MB and 12.2 × 10−3 min−1 for RhB after 210 min, respectively. The absorbance change at 665 nm for MB or 555 nm for RhB was also measured at different time intervals (Fig. 7b and d). The intensity of the absorption peaks gradually decreased, and the color of the reaction mixture faded in 210 min (Fig. S10). Therefore, CoCDs/TOCNF aerogels are efficient for the catalytic degradation of different organic dyes with H2O2.

To explore the catalytic mechanism of CoCDs/TOCNF aerogels for dye degradation, radical scavenging experiments were conducted by adding IPA and BQ individually to MO dye solutions (Fig. 7e), respectively. When IPA was added to MO, the degradation was decreased, since •OH can be captured by IPA [52,53], whereas a slight decrease was observed in the presence of BQ (•O2 − scavenger). This result again demonstrates the generation of ROS due to the POD-like activity of CoCDs. Then, these radicals cleave the dyes completely. Based on these findings, a plausible mechanism for MO, MB, and RhB degradation was proposed with CoCDs/TOCNF aerogels and H2O2 system (Fig. 7f).

Recycling and reuse are significant challenges in the practical application of catalysts. To explore the recyclability of CoCDs/TOCNF aerogel for MO, MB, and RhB degradation, five cycle experiments were conducted (Fig. 8a, b, and c). All experimental conditions were maintained at optimal levels to ensure reliable results. During the degradation after each cycle, the aerogels were withdrawn from the solution, thoroughly washed with deionized water, and reused for the next catalytic cycle. The degradation efficiency of CoCDs0.3/TOCNF aerogel remained above 90 % for all dyes even after five cycles. The reason for maintaining the high degradation efficiency of nanozyme even after multiple uses for dyes degradation is immobilization. This immobilization facilitated the recovery and reuse of CoCDs because their small size and high-water solubility make it difficult its separate and reuse. Furthermore, it prevented the leaching of CoCDs. However, CoCDs/TOCNF aerogels offer easy handling without the need for difficult techniques. They can be simply retrieved with tweezers, washed, and reused, presenting a practical solution for catalyst recovery.

Additionally, the leaching of CoCDs during dye degradation was evaluated by determining the Co content before and after catalysis using ICP-OES analysis. After five cycles of degradation, a 5.14 % decrease in Co content was observed, indicating small amounts of Co leaching in consecutive cycles. Furthermore, SEM images of CoCDs0.3/TOCNF aerogel after five cycles of MO degradation still showed a porous structure (Fig. 8d and e), which was attributed to the introduction of CoCDs into the TOCNF aerogel, serving as a rigid crosslinking agent to enhance compression strength and prevent the disintegration of the porous microstructure during recycling.

To assess the practical applicability of the CoCDs0.3/TOCNF aerogel, we evaluated its MO degradation performance in real-world river and lake water samples. As illustrated in Fig. 8f, 87.56 % of MO was degraded in lake water and 82.21 % in river water at pH 4.00. These values are slightly lower than the degradation observed in pH 4.00 buffer prepared with DW (93.07 %), likely due to the presence of interfering substances, such as anions or radical scavengers [46], which can diminish the catalytic efficiency of the nanozyme. Nevertheless, the high degradation efficiency in acidic conditions can be attributed to the enhanced POD-like activity of CoCDs. Acidic environments favor the Fenton-like reaction of cobalt-based materials, where H₂O₂ decomposes to generate highly reactive hydroxyl radicals (•OH) [26], leading to effective dye degradation. When using unbuffered filtered real water samples directly to prepare dye solutions, the removal efficiency significantly decreased to 4.87 % in lake water and 4.64 % in river water. This decline in efficiency can be attributed to the higher pH values of the lake (pH = 7.14) and river water (pH = 7.38) samples. It is crucial to note that the POD-like activity of the CoCDs nanozyme significantly diminishes above pH 4.00, resulting in reduced catalytic efficiency.

This study successfully synthesized CoCDs nanozymes exhibiting POD-like activity, as evidenced by their favorable kinetic parameters. These CoCDs were covalently bonded onto TOCNF-based aerogel via carboxyl amine condensation to produce recyclable nanozymes. The obtained aerogel with a CoCDs loading of 0.3 wt% displayed the highest grafting content, leading to optimal degradation efficiency. The developed aerogel demonstrated low density, high porosity, improved mechanical properties, and excellent shape recovery, attributed to the incorporation of CoCDs. The prepared aerogels exhibited exceptional degradation efficiency for various organic dyes, including MO, MB, and RhB. Furthermore, the aerogels exhibited remarkable recyclability, maintaining degradation performance for up to five cycles for all dyes. The synthesized nanozymes offer a promising reference for the development of highly active carbon dots-based nanozymes and hold potential applications in environmental protection. The simple separation method employed in this study can be adapted for various other technological applications, highlighting the versatility of these nanozymes.
